ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Kerala Beef Fry / Beef Ularthiyathu. Mapped to the broader 'beef dish' class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Kerala-style dry-roasted beef. A00GJ (Beef meat, cooked) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor; spice and coconut oil components are not captured.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Kerala Beef Fry or Beef Ularthiyathu as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Kerala Beef Fry is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a moderate side-dish portion served alongside Malabar Parotta, Appam, or steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods does not list Kerala Beef Fry as a composite dish entry. Values are adapted from NIN beef (cooked) and coconut oil component data, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef, chuck, braised (USDA NDB No. 13340) and coconut oil (USDA NDB No. 04047). Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy beef chuck braised profile. Micronutrient values retained from input enriched_json at confidence 0.9 and are consistent with NIN and USDA reference ranges for cooked beef with coconut oil.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kerala Beef Fry (Beef Ularthiyathu). GI estimated at 15 by analogy with cooked beef and similar low-carbohydrate meat dishes reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. With only 3.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a serving of 150 g (5.7 g carbohydrate), the glycemic load per serving is approximately 0.9, confirming negligible glycemic impact. GI confidence: 0.7 (estimated).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (320 mg/100 g) and significant saturated fat from beef and coconut oil
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g, reduce added salt during preparation, trim visible fat from the beef, and use a minimal amount of coconut oil to lower both sodium and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.8 g/100 g) and high protein (19.5 g/100 g) resulting in negligible glycemic impact (estimated GI 15, GL ~0.9 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kerala Beef Fry is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a protein-rich, low-glycemic side dish; however, total meal glycemic load should be monitored when served with high-GI accompaniments such as Malabar Parotta or white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~6.8 g/100 g) from beef and coconut oil, combined with dietary cholesterol (~72 mg/100 g) and high sodium (320 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Kerala Beef Fry only occasionally, limit portions to 80–100 g, choose lean cuts of beef, and reduce coconut oil quantity to minimise saturated fat and sodium load in line with AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat is pro-inflammatory in susceptible individuals due to saturated fat and arachidonic acid content; black pepper and ginger have anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit frequency of consumption of red meat dishes such as Kerala Beef Fry; the ginger and black pepper components may offer modest anti-inflammatory benefit but do not offset the pro-inflammatory potential of the beef base.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kerala Beef Fry, also known as Beef Ularthiyathu or Nadan Beef Fry, is a celebrated dry-roasted beef dish originating from the coastal state of Kerala in South India. The preparation follows a distinctive two-stage cooking process: beef cubes are first pressure-cooked with turmeric, salt, and ginger-garlic paste until tender, then transferred to a wok or kadai and slow-roasted in coconut oil with a coarse masala of fennel seeds, black peppercorns, and dried red chilies until the meat turns dark, dry, and intensely flavoured. A defining feature of the dish is the addition of Thenga Kothu — thin slices of fresh coconut — along with generous quantities of fresh curry leaves, which impart a characteristic crunch, nuttiness, and aromatic depth. The result is a dish with a firm, slightly chewy texture, a deeply spiced and smoky flavour, and a rich coconut-oil aroma. Nutritionally, Kerala Beef Fry is high in protein (19.5 g/100 g), moderate-to-high in fat (17.2 g/100 g, of which approximately 6.8 g is saturated), and very low in carbohydrates (3.8 g/100 g), making it a low-glycemic food with an estimated GI of 15. It is a rich source of zinc (4.5 mg), vitamin B12 (2.2 mcg), iron (2.8 mg), and niacin (4.2 mg). Sodium content is notable at 320 mg/100 g. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycemic load, but should be consumed with caution by those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its saturated fat and sodium content. It is traditionally served as a side dish with Malabar Parotta, Appam, or steamed rice, and is a cultural icon at Kerala festive meals and social gatherings.

confidence_reason: ICMR-NIN does not publish a composite dish entry for Kerala Beef Fry. Macronutrient values are adapted from NIN cooked beef and coconut oil component data, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy beef chuck braised profile. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and cholesterol are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data. Micronutrient values are from the input enriched_json (confidence 0.9) and are consistent with NIN and USDA reference ranges. GI is an analogous estimate. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: 1. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the Kerala Beef Fry composite dish (post pressure-cooking and dry-roasting) is not directly reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific two-stage preparation; detailed moisture data for this composite dish is unavailable in any approved source as of 2026. The dry-roasting stage (Ularthiyathu) drives off significant moisture but the precise residual water content of the finished dish has not been measured and published in an approved source. 2. Saturated fat (6.8 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (5.4 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9 g), trans_fat_g (0.2 g), and cholesterol_mg (72.0 mg) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy beef chuck braised (NDB No. 13340) and coconut oil (NDB No. 04047) component profiles, as ICMR-NIN does not report fatty acid fractions for this composite dish. 3. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables for cooked beef and low-carbohydrate meat dishes; no direct GI measurement for Kerala Beef Fry exists in the literature. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (15 × 5.7) / 100 = 0.9. 4. Halal status is marked false as Kerala Beef Fry is traditionally prepared in non-halal-certified contexts; halal-certified versions exist but cannot be assumed generically. 5. Portions in the input JSON contained four identical duplicate entries (150 g); deduplicated to a single canonical serving of 150 g. 6. Input aliases included 'கேரளா பீப் ப்ரை' (Tamil transliteration variant); this has been retained in the flat aliases array for retrieval coverage alongside the corrected Tamil native form 'கேரளா பீஃப் ஃப்ரை'. 7. No FoodOn exact match or SNOMED CT code found for Kerala Beef Fry; ontology mapped to broader defensible anchors with gap notes as required by G5.
